name: ECDLP verified knowledge graph
purpose: A machine-readable, navigable index of the machine-checked (Lean 4 + Mathlib, no sorry, no axioms) structure of the ECDLP landscape for secp256k1: proved theorems, their import dependencies, the corpus claims they verify, and the formalization barriers that bound them. Built for a future automated reasoner to load the known-and-verified frontier without re-deriving it.
invariant: Every theorem listed is accepted by the Lean kernel with no sorry and no added axioms. Truth is decided only by the kernel; this graph is derived from the ledger, it does not assert proofs.

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Curve.secp256k1_classical_security_profile
theorems.claim: secp256k1 classical attack-resistance profile ‚Äî the substrate thesis, one unconditional theorem (`secp256k1_classical_security_profile`: a single kernel-verified conjunction certifying that *every known classically-expressible ECDLP shortcut is blocked* ‚Äî `#E = n` (cofactor 1) ‚àß `Nat.Prime n` (‚áí no Pohlig‚ÄìHellman reduction, no proper subgroup) ‚àß `t ‚âÝ 0` (not supersingular) ‚àß `t ‚âÝ 1` (not anomalous) ‚àß `t ‚â§ 4p` (Hasse) ‚àß embedding degree `> 100` (no MOV/FR transfer). Honest scope: this is the *classical structural* envelope, not a hardness proof ‚Äî the only proved hardness is the generic `Œ©(‚àön)` bound (`secp256k1_generic_security`), and it is classical-only (Shor breaks ECDLP quantumly). The machine-checked statement of "every classical attack we can express is blocked", not "unbreakable". Assembles proved conjuncts; inherits `Lean.ofReduceBool` from them)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Curve.secp256k1_twist_security_profile`, `secp256k1_twist_order_factorization`, `secp256k1_twist_maxprime_prime
theorems.claim: secp256k1 quadratic-twist security certificate ‚Äî a limitation, not a strength (`secp256k1_twist_security_profile`: the twist order `#·∫º = 2p+2‚àín` factors exactly as `3¬∑13¬∑3319¬∑22639¬∑Q` with `Q` a 220-bit prime (`secp256k1_twist_maxprime_prime`, full Pratt certificate; `secp256k1_twist_order_factorization`). Two honest consequences: (1) the cofactor `3¬∑13¬∑3319¬∑22639 = 114286177161 ‚âà 2¬≥‚Å∑ > 1` is nontrivial ‚Äî unlike the simple point group of `E`, the twist has genuine small-order subgroups a point can be confined to; (2) `2‚Åπ < Q < 2‚Å∞`, so generic twist-DLP costs `‚âà ‚àöQ < 2‚Å∞`, below the curve's `‚âà2‚Å∏`. This is the machine-checked reason single-coordinate (`x`-only / Montgomery-ladder) secp256k1 code must validate points. Inherits `Lean.ofReduceBool` from `native_decide` + Pratt)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Curve.secp256k1_frobenius_norm`, `secp256k1_frobenius_trace`, `secp256k1_four_p_eq_trace_sq

theorems.claim: Point decomposition ‚áí Semaev relation ‚Äî doubling case (`secp256k1_point_decomposition_semaev_double`: the `P‚ÇÅ = P‚ÇÇ` companion ‚Äî if `R = 2¬∑P‚ÇÅ` with `P‚ÇÅ` not 2-torsion, then `S‚ÇÉ(x(P‚ÇÅ), x(P‚ÇÅ), x(R)) = 0`. With the generic-sum theorem, covers every 2-term decomposition of `R`)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Semaev.secp256k1_S‚ÇÉpoly_natDegree
theorems.claim: Semaev polynomial has degree exactly 2 in each variable (`secp256k1_S‚ÇÉpoly_natDegree`: for `x‚ÇÅ ‚âÝ x‚ÇÇ` over `ùîΩ_p`, `S‚ÇÉ(x‚ÇÅ,x‚ÇÇ,¬∑)` is a degree-exactly-2 univariate polynomial, leading coefficient `(x‚ÇÅ‚àíx‚ÇÇ) ‚âÝ 0`. Base case of the classical `deg_{x·µ¢} S‚Çò = 2^{m‚àí2}` degree tower ‚Äî the doubling that makes higher-order decompositions blow up. Via `Polynomial.natDegree_quadratic`)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Semaev.secp256k1_decomposition_completions_le_two
theorems.claim: Bounded decomposition fan-out (index-calculus cost ingredient) (`secp256k1_decomposition_completions_le_two`: fixing a base coordinate `x‚ÇÅ` and the target `x_R` with `x‚ÇÅ ‚âÝ x_R`, at most 2 field elements `x‚ÇÇ` complete a 2-decomposition `S‚ÇÉ(x‚ÇÅ,x‚ÇÇ,x_R)=0` ‚Äî any finite set of completions has card `‚â§ 2`. A degree-2 polynomial over a field has `‚â§ 2` roots, so the index-calculus relation graph has bounded fan-out at each factor-base point. The one exact, kernel-checked quantitative fact under the prime-field cost analysis ‚Äî *not* the hardness claim itself, which stays an open conjecture; see `BARRIERS.md`)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Semaev.S‚ÇÉ_root_of_eq_zero
theorems.claim: Semaev's `S‚ÇÉ` ‚Äî reverse direction (the two roots) (`S‚ÇÉ_root_of_eq_zero`: `S‚ÇÉ` is quadratic in `x‚ÇÉ` with leading coefficient `(x‚ÇÅ‚àíx‚ÇÇ)`; if `(x‚ÇÅ,y‚ÇÅ), (x‚ÇÇ,y‚ÇÇ)` lie on `y = x¬≥ + a¬∑x + b` and `S‚ÇÉ(x‚ÇÅ,x‚ÇÇ,x‚ÇÉ) = 0`, then `(x‚ÇÅ‚àíx‚ÇÇ)¬∑x‚ÇÉ` equals `(y‚ÇÇ‚àíy‚ÇÅ) ‚àí (x‚ÇÅ+x‚ÇÇ)(x‚ÇÅ‚àíx‚ÇÇ)` or `(y‚ÇÇ+y‚ÇÅ) ‚àí (x‚ÇÅ+x‚ÇÇ)(x‚ÇÅ‚àíx‚ÇÇ)` ‚Äî the cleared `x`-coordinates of `P‚ÇÅ+P‚ÇÇ` and `P‚ÇÅ‚àíP‚ÇÇ`. Via a sympy-certified master factorization `(x‚ÇÅ‚àíx‚ÇÇ)¬∑S‚ÇÉ = (D¬∑x‚ÇÉ‚àíR‚Çä)(D¬∑x‚ÇÉ‚àíR‚Çã)`, a pure `ring` identity, then `mul_eq_zero`; needs no `x‚ÇÅ ‚âÝ x‚ÇÇ`)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Semaev.S‚ÇÉ_eq_zero_iff`, `secp256k1_semaev_three_iff
theorems.claim: Semaev's `S‚ÇÉ` ‚Äî full characterization (iff) (`S‚ÇÉ_eq_zero_iff`: for on-curve `(x‚ÇÅ,y‚ÇÅ), (x‚ÇÇ,y‚ÇÇ)` with `x‚ÇÅ ‚âÝ x‚ÇÇ`, `S‚ÇÉ(x‚ÇÅ,x‚ÇÇ,x‚ÇÉ) = 0 ‚ü∫ (x‚ÇÅ‚àíx‚ÇÇ)(x‚ÇÅ+x‚ÇÇ+x‚ÇÉ) = (y‚ÇÇ‚àíy‚ÇÅ)  ‚à®  = (y‚ÇÇ+y‚ÇÅ)` ‚Äî i.e. `x‚ÇÉ` is the `x`-coordinate of `P‚ÇÅ+P‚ÇÇ` or of `P‚ÇÅ‚àíP‚ÇÇ`; `secp256k1_semaev_three_iff` specializes to secp256k1. Combines forward + reverse: the complete statement of `S‚ÇÉ` as the elimination of `y‚ÇÅ,y‚ÇÇ` from "three points sum to `O`")

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Semaev.S‚ÇÑ`, `S‚ÇÑ_eq_zero_of_common_root`, `secp256k1_semaev_four_of_common_root
theorems.claim: Semaev's 4th summation polynomial `S‚ÇÑ` ‚Äî definition + forward direction (`S‚ÇÑ = Res_X(S‚ÇÉ(x‚ÇÅ,x‚ÇÇ,X), S‚ÇÉ(x‚ÇÉ,x‚ÇÑ,X))` via Mathlib's `Polynomial.resultant _ _ 2 2`; `S‚ÇÑ_eq_zero_of_common_root`: a shared root `X‚ÇÄ` of the two `S‚ÇÉ` slices ‚Äî e.g. `X‚ÇÄ = x(P‚ÇÅ+P‚ÇÇ) = x(P‚ÇÉ+P‚ÇÑ)` ‚Äî forces `S‚ÇÑ = 0`, over any commutative ring, via the resultant B√©zout identity `exists_mul_add_mul_eq_C_resultant`. `secp256k1_semaev_four_of_common_root` specializes to secp256k1. The first rung above `S‚ÇÉ` ‚Äî the recursion index calculus over `ùîΩ_{p^k}` uses. First `S‚ÇÑ` in Lean)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Semaev.resultant_eq_zero_of_common_root
theorems.claim: Semaev forward direction generalized to all `n` ‚Äî the resultant recursion engine (`resultant_eq_zero_of_common_root`: for *any* two univariate polynomials `f, g` over a commutative ring sharing an evaluation-root `X‚ÇÄ` (`f(X‚ÇÄ) = g(X‚ÇÄ) = 0`), `Res(f,g,m,n) = 0`, provided the Sylvester sizes are not both zero. Every Semaev step `S‚Çô‚Çä‚ÇÅ = Res_X(S‚Çô(‚Ä¶,X), S‚ÇÉ(‚Ä¶,X))` inherits its forward direction from this one lemma at level `n`; `S‚ÇÑ_eq_zero_of_common_root` is now literally the instance `f = S‚ÇÉ(x‚ÇÅ,x‚ÇÇ,¬∑)`, `g = S‚ÇÉ(x‚ÇÉ,x‚ÇÑ,¬∑)`, and `S‚ÇÖ, S‚ÇÜ, ‚Ä¶` are discharged verbatim with `f` the previous slice. The mechanism-level generalization of the Semaev forward direction to the whole infinite `S‚Çô` family ‚Äî via the B√©zout identity `exists_mul_add_mul_eq_C_resultant`, over any commutative ring, no field or closure)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Semaev.S‚ÇÉpoly_eval`, `S‚ÇÑ_block_swap`, `S‚ÇÑ_symm‚ÇÅ‚ÇÇ`, `S‚ÇÑ_symm‚ÇÉ‚ÇÑ
theorems.claim: *(alternate/supporting)* `S‚ÇÑ` infrastructure ‚Äî the `S‚ÇÉ`-as-polynomial bridge `S‚ÇÉpoly_eval` (`(S‚ÇÉpoly ‚Ä¶).eval x‚ÇÉ = S‚ÇÉ ‚Ä¶`) and `S‚ÇÑ`'s full symmetry: block swap `S‚ÇÑ_block_swap` `(x‚ÇÅ,x‚ÇÇ)‚Üî(x‚ÇÉ,x‚ÇÑ)` (from `resultant_comm`) and within-pair `S‚ÇÑ_symm‚ÇÅ‚ÇÇ`, `S‚ÇÑ_symm‚ÇÉ‚ÇÑ` ‚Äî so `S‚ÇÑ` is symmetric on all of `{x‚ÇÅ,x‚ÇÇ,x‚ÇÉ,x‚ÇÑ}`, the symmetry a summation polynomial must have

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Semaev.S‚ÇÑ_common_root_of_eq_zero`, `secp256k1_semaev_four_common_root_of_eq_zero
theorems.claim: Semaev's `S‚ÇÑ` ‚Äî reverse/meaning direction (`S‚ÇÑ_common_root_of_eq_zero`: over a field, for on-curve `(x‚ÇÅ,y‚ÇÅ), (x‚ÇÇ,y‚ÇÇ)` with `x‚ÇÅ ‚âÝ x‚ÇÇ` and `S‚ÇÑ(x‚ÇÅ,x‚ÇÇ,x‚ÇÉ,x‚ÇÑ) = 0`, the two `S‚ÇÉ` slices share a root `X‚ÇÄ` in the field ‚Äî the converse of `S‚ÇÑ_eq_zero_of_common_root`. Since `S‚ÇÑ` is the resultant, its vanishing forces a shared root; it lies *in* `K` because `S‚ÇÉ(x‚ÇÅ,x‚ÇÇ,¬∑)` splits with the explicitly known roots `x(P‚ÇÅ¬±P‚ÇÇ)`. Via `resultant_eq_prod_eval`; `secp256k1_semaev_four_common_root_of_eq_zero` specializes. With the forward direction, `S‚ÇÑ` now holds both ways)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Semaev.S‚ÇÉpoly_master_factor
theorems.claim: S‚ÇÉ`-slice two-root factorization over `F[X]` (`S‚ÇÉpoly_master_factor`: `(x‚ÇÅ‚àíx‚ÇÇ)¬∑S‚ÇÉpoly = (D¬∑X ‚àí R‚Çä)¬∑(D¬∑X ‚àí R‚Çã)` as a polynomial identity in `F[X]` ‚Äî the lift of the scalar master factorization, exhibiting `S‚ÇÉ`'s degree-2 slice as split with the explicit roots `x(P‚ÇÅ¬±P‚ÇÇ)`; the key input to the `S‚ÇÑ` reverse)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Semaev.S‚ÇÉ_symm‚ÇÅ‚ÇÇ`, `S‚ÇÉ_symm‚ÇÇ‚ÇÉ
theorems.claim: *(alternate/supporting)* Semaev `S‚ÇÉ` full symmetry ‚Äî `S‚ÇÉ_symm‚ÇÅ‚ÇÇ` (swap `x‚ÇÅ,x‚ÇÇ`) and `S‚ÇÉ_symm‚ÇÇ‚ÇÉ` (swap `x‚ÇÇ,x‚ÇÉ`), so `S‚ÇÉ` is symmetric in all three `x`-arguments, as a summation polynomial must be

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Curve.secp256k1_double_x_eq_Œ¶‚ÇÇ_div_Œ®‚ÇÇSq`, `secp256k1_Œ¶‚ÇÇ
theorems.claim: Multiplication-by-2 `x`-coordinate formula in Mathlib's canonical division polynomials (`secp256k1_double_x_eq_Œ¶‚ÇÇ_div_Œ®‚ÇÇSq`: for a non-2-torsion point `P=(x,y)` on `y=x¬≥+7`, the doubled `x`-coordinate `addX x x (slope x x y y) = (Œ¶ 2)(x)/(Œ®‚ÇÇSq)(x)` ‚Äî with Mathlib's own `Œ¶`/`Œ®Sq` division polynomials, `Œ¶ 2 = X‚Å¥‚àí56X` (`secp256k1_Œ¶‚ÇÇ`), `Œ®‚ÇÇSq = 4X¬≥+28 = 4y`. This is the base case `n=2` of the general multiplication formula `x([n]P)=Œ¶‚Çô/Œ®Sq‚Çô` ‚Äî the engine of the general `œà‚Çô`-vanishing ‚ü∫ `n`-torsion bridge (`notes/FOUNDATIONS.md` rung 4). Unlike the per-`n` bridges (which used ad-hoc reduced polynomials like `3x‚Å¥+84x`), this states the *canonical* `Œ¶‚Çô/Œ®Sq‚Çô` shape. The general-`n` formula, by induction on the division-polynomial recurrence, is the missing Mathlib rung ‚Äî absent from v4.31, present only in a stalled upstream PR)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Weil.secp256k1_torsion_iff_principal
theorems.claim: Weil-pairing foundations, rung 1 ‚Äî torsion ‚ü∫ principal divisor (`secp256k1_torsion_iff_principal`: a point `P` of secp256k1 is `n`-torsion `‚ü∫ n ‚Ä¢ toClass P = 0`, i.e. iff the divisor `n¬∑([P] ‚àí [O])` is principal in the coordinate ring `F[secp256k1]`. Obtained by transporting `n`-torsion across Mathlib's Abel‚ÄìJacobi map `toClass : W.Point ‚Üí+ Additive (ClassGroup W.CoordinateRing)` ‚Äî the injective embedding `E ‚Ü™ Pic` on which Mathlib *builds* the group law ‚Äî via `toClass_eq_zero` + `map_nsmul`. This is the first genuine rung of the Weil pairing construction: principality of `n¬∑([P] ‚àí [O])` is the existence precondition for the Miller function `f_P`. Key finding: the divisor-class substrate (`toClass`, `FunctionField`, `rootsOfUnity`) is already in Mathlib, so the pairing is more reachable than "from zero" ‚Äî remaining rungs (extract `f_P`, evaluate at divisors, Weil reciprocity, bilinear `e‚Çô‚ÜíŒº‚Çô`) are the open frontier, `notes/FOUNDATIONS.md`)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Weil.secp256k1_miller_function_exists
theorems.claim: Weil-pairing foundations, rung 2 ‚Äî the Miller function exists (`secp256k1_miller_function_exists`: for a nonzero `n`-torsion point `P=(x,y)` of secp256k1, there exists `f_P` in the function field `F(secp256k1)` generating the principal fractional ideal `(‚ü®X‚àíx, Y‚àíy‚ü©)‚Åø = (XYIdeal' h)‚Åø` ‚Äî the Miller function, whose divisor is `n¬∑([P] ‚àí [O])`. Extracted from rung 1: `n`-torsion trivializes the class `ClassGroup.mk ((XYIdeal' h)‚Åø) = (mk (XYIdeal' h))‚Åø = 1`, and Mathlib's `ClassGroup.mk_eq_one_iff` turns a trivial class into a principal ideal, whose generator is `f_P`. Rung W2 of the Weil sub-ladder ‚Äî the input to Weil reciprocity / the pairing `e‚Çô`)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Weil.secp256k1_miller_function_unique
theorems.claim: Weil-pairing foundations, rung 3 ‚Äî the Miller function is unique up to a unit (`secp256k1_miller_function_unique`: two generators `f, g` of the same principal ideal `(XYIdeal' h)‚Åø` differ by a unit of the coordinate ring, `‚àÉ u : F[secp256k1]À£, u ‚Ä¢ f = g` ‚Äî the well-definedness input for the Weil pairing `e‚Çô` (its value must not depend on the chosen Miller function). Representative-independence half of rung W3. Proof designed by the Fable model and kernel-verified ‚Äî a piloted "strong-model + Lean-kernel" loop: Fable read the Mathlib source, identified `Submodule.span_singleton_eq_span_singleton`, and produced the proof, which the kernel accepted)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Weil.evalAt`, `evalAt_surjective`, `evalAt_ker
theorems.claim: Weil-pairing infrastructure ‚Äî point-evaluation homomorphism (`evalAt`: for a rational point `(x,y)` on any Weierstrass curve over a field, evaluation of a regular function at `P` as a ring hom `F[E] ‚Üí+* F`, built from Mathlib's `quotientXYIdealEquiv` (`F[E]/‚ü®X‚àíx,Y‚àíy‚ü© ‚âÉ F`); `evalAt_surjective` (every value attained ‚Äî `P` is `F`-rational) and `evalAt_ker` (kernel `= ‚ü®X‚àíx, Y‚àíy‚ü©`, the maximal ideal of `P` ‚Äî the functions vanishing at `P`). The first rung of the function-evaluation layer Mathlib lacks (v4.31 has no rational-function evaluation API and `F[E]` is not known to be Dedekind there); regular-function evaluation now exists, the rational extension via localization is next. Unblocks the Weil pairing's `f_P(D_Q)` step)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Weil.xyIdeal_isMaximal
theorems.claim: A rational point is a closed point (maximal ideal) (`xyIdeal_isMaximal`: for `(x,y)` on any Weierstrass curve over a field, the ideal `‚ü®X‚àíx, Y‚àíy‚ü©` of `F[E]` is maximal ‚Äî since the quotient `F[E]/‚ü®X‚àíx,Y‚àíy‚ü© ‚âÉ F` is a field. Establishes each `F`-rational point as a closed point of the affine curve; the prerequisite for localizing at `P` (the local ring `F[E]_P`) to evaluate rational functions regular at `P` ‚Äî the next infrastructure rung toward the Weil pairing's `f_P(D_Q)`)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Weil.evalRatAt_algebraMap
theorems.claim: evalRatAt` extends `evalAt` (`evalRatAt_algebraMap`: for `(x,y)` on any Weierstrass curve over a field and a regular function `r ‚àà F[E]`, the value of its image in the local ring `F[E]_P = Localization.AtPrime ‚ü®X‚àíx,Y‚àíy‚ü©` under `evalRatAt` equals its direct value `evalAt h r`. The correctness certificate that rational-function evaluation genuinely extends regular-function evaluation ‚Äî the residue of the localization at `P` coincides *definitionally* with the quotient by `P`'s maximal ideal. Closes the evaluation layer before the Miller function `f_P` is evaluated; proof developed on the warm-server `lake env lean` loop)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Weil.xyIdeal_ne_of_x_ne
theorems.claim: Distinct x-coordinates ‚áí distinct closed points (`xyIdeal_ne_of_x_ne`: rational points on a Weierstrass curve with `x‚ÇÅ ‚âÝ x‚ÇÇ` give different maximal ideals `‚ü®X‚àíx, Y‚àíy‚ü©` of `F[E]`. Proof: `XClass x‚ÇÅ ‚àí XClass x‚ÇÇ = algebraMap F F[E] (x‚ÇÇ‚àíx‚ÇÅ)` is a unit when `x‚ÇÅ ‚âÝ x‚ÇÇ`; equal ideals would force that unit into a maximal (proper) ideal ‚Äî contradiction. The divisor-support separation the Weil pairing needs ‚Äî the points of `D_Q` chosen to compute `f_P(D_Q)` must avoid `P`, `O`, which requires distinct points to be genuinely distinct closed points; scouted + proved on the warm-server loop)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Weil.xyIdeal_ne_of_ne` (+ `xyIdeal_ne_of_y_ne`)
theorems.claim: Distinct points ‚áí distinct closed points (full separation) (`xyIdeal_ne_of_ne`: for rational points `(x‚ÇÅ,y‚ÇÅ) ‚âÝ (x‚ÇÇ,y‚ÇÇ)` on a Weierstrass curve the maximal ideals `‚ü®X‚àíx, Y‚àíy‚ü©` differ ‚Äî combining the x-coordinate case with the `YClass` companion `xyIdeal_ne_of_y_ne` (`y‚ÇÅ ‚âÝ y‚ÇÇ ‚áí` distinct ideals, same unit-in-a-maximal-ideal argument on `Y ‚àí ¬∑`). The complete divisor-support-separation lemma for the Weil pairing: any two distinct rational points are distinct closed points of `F[E]`)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Weil.evalRatAt_eq_zero_iff
theorems.claim: Weil W3 ‚Äî zero locus of point-evaluation (`evalRatAt_eq_zero_iff`: a rational function regular at `P` vanishes at `P` iff it lies in the maximal ideal of the local ring `F[E]_P`. From `evalRatAt = residueFieldEquiv ‚àò residue`: residue-field iso injective, so `evalRatAt r = 0 ‚Üî residue r = 0 ‚Üî r ‚àà ùî™`)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Weil.evalRatAt_ne_zero_iff_isUnit
theorems.claim: Weil W3 ‚Äî nonvanishing criterion at a point (`evalRatAt_ne_zero_iff_isUnit`: a rational function regular at `P` is nonzero at `P` iff it is a unit of the local ring `F[E]_P` ‚Äî exactly the condition to evaluate a Miller function `f_P` to a nonzero value in `ùîΩ_p`, off its zero/pole support. Completes the *point-evaluation* half of Weil rung W3; `f_P(D_Q)` over a whole divisor and Weil reciprocity (W4) remain genuine Mathlib gaps ‚Äî see `BARRIERS.md`)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Weil.secp256k1_psi3_root_iff_class_torsion
theorems.claim: œà‚ÇÉ root ‚ü∫ Miller divisor `3¬∑([P]‚àí[O])` principal (`secp256k1_psi3_root_iff_class_torsion`: for a nonzero affine `P=(x,y)` of secp256k1, `(œà 3).evalEval x y = 0 ‚Üî 3 ‚Ä¢ toClass P = 0` ‚Äî the 3-division polynomial vanishes at `P` iff the Abel‚ÄìJacobi class is 3-torsion, i.e. iff `3¬∑([P]‚àí[O])` is principal. Welds the division-polynomial torsion tower to Weil divisor-class rung 1 at their shared middle `3‚Ä¢P=0`; the first result letting a computable coordinate condition `œà 3 = 0` *decide* the divisor-theoretic principality the Miller function needs)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Weil.secp256k1_psi5_root_iff_class_torsion
theorems.claim: œà‚ÇÖ root ‚ü∫ Miller divisor `5¬∑([P]‚àí[O])` principal (`secp256k1_psi5_root_iff_class_torsion`: the `n=5` analogue ‚Äî `(œà 5).evalEval x y = 0 ‚Üî 5 ‚Ä¢ toClass P = 0`)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Weil.secp256k1_psi7_root_iff_class_torsion
theorems.claim: œà‚Çá root ‚ü∫ Miller divisor `7¬∑([P]‚àí[O])` principal (`secp256k1_psi7_root_iff_class_torsion`: the `n=7` analogue ‚Äî `(œà 7).evalEval x y = 0 ‚Üî 7 ‚Ä¢ toClass P = 0`)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Curve.secp256k1_grp_nsmul_eq_zero` (+ the `Module (ZMod n) ‚Ü•‚ü®G‚ü©` instance, `secp256k1G‚Çô`)
theorems.claim: The concrete secp256k1 subgroup `‚ü®G‚ü©` is a `ZMod n`-module (`secp256k1_grp_nsmul_eq_zero`: every point of `‚ü®G‚ü© = zmultiples secp256k1G` is annihilated by `n`, so `‚ü®G‚ü©` carries a `Module (ZMod n)` structure built via `AddCommGroup.zmodModule`. This realises the abstract discrete-log model on a genuine elliptic-curve group ‚Äî Mathlib provides no automatic `ZMod n`-module on a cyclic subgroup; it must be constructed from the exponent bound, which the weak keystone `addOrderOf secp256k1G = n` supplies. The bridge that lets every abstract protocol theorem instantiate on real `WeierstrassCurve.Affine.Point` values)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Curve.secp256k1_dh_agree`, `secp256k1_schnorr_verify`, `secp256k1_taproot_tweak_verify`, `secp256k1_adaptor_complete
theorems.claim: Deployed signature/agreement protocols are correct on the concrete secp256k1 group (the abstract `Ecdlp.Schnorr` correctness theorems instantiated at the concrete carrier `‚Ü•‚ü®G‚ü©` with base point `secp256k1G‚Çô`, about genuine curve points: Diffie‚ÄìHellman agreement `secp256k1_dh_agree`, Schnorr/EdDSA verification `secp256k1_schnorr_verify`, Bitcoin Taproot key-tweak `secp256k1_taproot_tweak_verify` (BIP-341), adaptor-signature completeness `secp256k1_adaptor_complete` (atomic swaps / Lightning PTLCs). Contingent only on `ord(G) = n` + the abstract algebra ‚Äî the honest content: the real secp256k1 protocols verify on the actual curve subgroup, not only an abstract module)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Curve.secp256k1_threshold_schnorr_aggregate`, `secp256k1_musig_key_aggregate`, `secp256k1_feldman_vss_verify`, `secp256k1_threshold_elgamal_combine`, `secp256k1_schnorr_batch_verify
theorems.claim: *(instantiation, multiparty protocols)* the same concrete-`‚ü®G‚ü©` realization for the multi-signer / threshold protocols ‚Äî aggregate Schnorr / Taproot multisig `secp256k1_threshold_schnorr_aggregate` (MuSig/FROST), MuSig2 key aggregation `secp256k1_musig_key_aggregate`, Feldman VSS share verification `secp256k1_feldman_vss_verify` (DKG), threshold ElGamal partial-decryption combination `secp256k1_threshold_elgamal_combine`, batch Schnorr verification `secp256k1_schnorr_batch_verify

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Curve.secp256k1_grp_card`, `secp256k1_grp_isAddCyclic
theorems.claim: The secp256k1 crypto subgroup `‚ü®G‚ü©` is cyclic of order exactly `n` (`secp256k1_grp_card`: `Nat.card ‚ü®G‚ü© = n`, from `Nat.card (zmultiples g) = addOrderOf g` + the weak keystone; `secp256k1_grp_isAddCyclic`: `IsAddCyclic ‚ü®G‚ü©`. The group-object-level statement of "‚ü®G‚ü© is a concrete cyclic group of order `n`" ‚Äî no Hasse/`#E`, only `addOrderOf G = n`)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Curve.secp256k1_dlogEquiv` (+ `secp256k1_dlogHom`, `_injective`, `_surjective`, `secp256k1_gen_addOrderOf`)
theorems.claim: The discrete-log map is a group isomorphism `ZMod n ‚âÉ+ ‚ü®G‚ü©` (`secp256k1_dlogEquiv`: base-point multiplication `c ‚Ü¶ c¬∑G` is an additive-group isomorphism between the scalar ring `ZMod n` and the concrete secp256k1 subgroup `‚ü®G‚ü©` ‚Äî the exact group-theoretic content of the ECDLP setting: the discrete log `dlog = (c ‚Ü¶ c¬∑G)‚Åª` is a well-defined bijection. Its *computational* inversion is the open ECDLP hardness conjecture; this asserts only the unconditional bijection, via injective (`c.val < n = addOrderOf G`) + surjective (`zmultiples`) on the `ZMod n`-module `‚ü®G‚ü©`. Built with `AddEquiv.ofBijective`)

theorems.name: WeierstrassCurve.Affine.instFinitePoint
theorems.claim: Affine Weierstrass points are finite over any finite ring ‚Äî general lemma (Mathlib-gap upstream candidate) (`instFinitePoint`: `Finite W.Point` for every `W : WeierstrassCurve.Affine R` with `[CommRing R] [Finite R]`, via `Point ‚Ü™ Option (R √ó R)`. Weakest hypotheses ‚Äî no `Field`/`Nontrivial`. Mathlib v4.31.0 has no `Finite`/`Fintype` instance on any `Point` variant (gap confirmed against pinned source); natural upstream home `Mathlib/AlgebraicGeometry/EllipticCurve/Affine/Point.lean`. Subsumes the two per-curve instances below, now one-line `inferInstance` corollaries. Pure-kernel)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Curve.instFiniteSecp256k1Point
theorems.claim: secp256k1 rational-point group is finite (`instFiniteSecp256k1Point`: `Finite secp256k1.toAffine.Point`, now a one-line `inferInstance` corollary of the general `WeierstrassCurve.Affine.instFinitePoint` since `ZMod Secp256k1.p` is a finite commutative ring. Makes Mathlib's `Nat.card E.toAffine.Point` (as used in `EllipticCurve/LFunction.lean`) a real quantity rather than junk `0`. Pure-kernel, no native_decide)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Curve.secp256k1_n_dvd_card_point
theorems.claim: n ‚à£ #E(ùîΩ_p)` ‚Äî base-point order divides the curve cardinality (`secp256k1_n_dvd_card_point`: `Secp256k1.n ‚à£ Nat.card secp256k1.toAffine.Point`, by Lagrange on `‚ü®G‚ü©` (order the proved `n`) + finiteness. The first verified fact about `#E` itself ‚Äî all prior point-counting stayed inside `‚ü®G‚ü©`. Pins `#E ‚àà {n, 2n, 3n, ‚Ä¶}`; the strong keystone `#E = n` is now proved on top, curve-specifically without Hasse/Schoof (`CurveCardinalityExact.lean`). Compiler-trusted: inherits `Lean.ofReduceBool` from the order keystone `secp256k1_generator_addOrderOf` via `secp256k1_grp_card` ‚Äî the Lagrange/finiteness step itself adds no `native_decide`)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Curve.secp256k1_card_point_eq_n`, `secp256k1_no_nonzero_two_torsion`, `secp256k1_card_point_le
theorems.claim: The exact curve cardinality `#E(ùîΩ_p) = n` ‚Äî cofactor 1, without Hasse or Schoof (`secp256k1_card_point_eq_n`: `Nat.card secp256k1.toAffine.Point = Secp256k1.n`). A curve-specific certificate that upgrades `n ‚à£ #E` to equality using the special structure of `E : Y = X¬≥ + 7` over `p ‚â° 1 (mod 3)`: `#E ‚â§ 2p+1` (`secp256k1_card_point_le`, ‚â§2 `y` per `x`) and `2p+1 < 3n` force `#E ‚àà {n, 2n}`; `2n` (even) is excluded because additive Cauchy would yield a nonzero 2-torsion point, but `E[2] = {O}` (`secp256k1_no_nonzero_two_torsion`: `2‚Ä¢P=0 ‚üπ P=0`, since `y=0 ‚üπ x¬≥=‚àí7` and `‚àí7` is not a cube in `ùîΩ_p`, `(‚àí7)^((p‚àí1)/3)‚âÝ1`). Removes the last standing assumption behind Units 1‚Äì2 (the published order `n` as `#E`). Uses `native_decide` for the 256-bit non-cube leaf and the literal arithmetic (`Lean.ofReduceBool`); numeric facts independently re-checked in `scripts/certs/cardinality_secp256k1.py`, prints `CERT_OK`)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Curve.secp256k1_grp_eq_top`, `secp256k1_point_isAddCyclic`, `secp256k1_mem_zmultiples`, `secp256k1_glvHom_eq_zsmul_unconditional
theorems.claim: The whole point group equals `‚ü®G‚ü©` ‚Äî `E(ùîΩ_p)` is cyclic, `glvPoint = [Œª]` unconditional (`secp256k1_grp_eq_top`: `secp256k1Grp = ‚ä§`, from `#‚ü®G‚ü© = n = #E` via `AddSubgroup.eq_top_of_card_eq`). Upgrades every `‚ü®G‚ü©`-only result to the full rational-point group now that `#E = n` is proved: `secp256k1_point_isAddCyclic` (`IsAddCyclic E(ùîΩ_p)`, an instance), `secp256k1_mem_zmultiples` (every point is a multiple of `G`), and `secp256k1_glvHom_eq_zsmul_unconditional` (the GLV eigenvalue property with the `[IsAddCyclic]` hypothesis now discharged ‚Äî `glvHom = [k]` with `k+k+1` annihilating every point, no remaining hypotheses). Resolves the `‚ü®G‚ü©`-vs-`E(ùîΩ_p)` scope ambiguity. Pure-kernel on top of the keystone

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Curve.secp256k1_pointGroupEquiv` (+ `secp256k1_point_group_equiv_exists`)
theorems.claim: The full point-group structure theorem: `E(ùîΩ_p) ‚âÉ+ ‚Ñ§/n` (`secp256k1_pointGroupEquiv`: base-point multiplication `c ‚Ü¶ c¬∑G` is an additive-group isomorphism from the scalar ring `ZMod n` onto the entire rational-point group `E(ùîΩ_p)`, not merely `‚ü®G‚ü©`. Lifts the subgroup discrete-log iso `secp256k1_dlogEquiv` off `‚ü®G‚ü©` by transporting across cofactor 1 `‚ü®G‚ü© = ‚ä§` (`AddEquiv.addSubgroupCongr secp256k1_grp_eq_top`) then stripping the `‚ä§` subtype (`AddSubgroup.topEquiv`). The complete group-theoretic classification of secp256k1's points: cyclic of order `n`, i.e. `‚âÖ ‚Ñ§/n`. Unconditional given `#E = n`; computational inversion is the open ECDLP conjecture, the bijection is a theorem. Inherits `Lean.ofReduceBool` through the order keystone)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Curve.secp256k1_point_group_no_proper_subgroup`, `secp256k1_nonzero_point_generates
theorems.claim: secp256k1's point group has no proper nontrivial subgroup ‚Äî group-level cofactor-1 security (`secp256k1_point_group_no_proper_subgroup`: every `AddSubgroup` of `E(ùîΩ_p)` is `‚ä•` or `‚ä§`, since `#E = n` is prime; plus `secp256k1_nonzero_point_generates`: every `P ‚âÝ 0` has `zmultiples P = ‚ä§`, and an `IsSimpleAddGroup` instance). The point-group (curve) statement of small-subgroup / invalid-subgroup attack resistance ‚Äî there is no small-order subgroup a point could be confined to. The geometric counterpart of the scalar-ring `secp256k1_scalar_no_proper_subgroup`, proved the same way with `secp256k1_card_point_eq_n` as the cardinality bridge. Pure-kernel on top of the keystone

theorems.name: Ecdlp.NormEDS.normEDS_shift_mul_shift_of_eq_zero`, `normEDS_sub_eq_zero_of_eq_zero
theorems.claim: Translation identity at an EDS zero + the `œÅ`-descent (`normEDS_shift_mul_shift_of_eq_zero`: if `W œÅ = 0` for `W = normEDS b c d` over any `CommRing`, then `W(n+œÅ)¬∑W(n‚àíœÅ) = ‚àíW(œÅ+1)¬∑W(œÅ‚àí1)¬∑W(n)` for every `n` ‚Äî the `r = 1` slice of `IsEllSequence` at a zero position, Ward's rank-of-apparition tool; and `normEDS_sub_eq_zero_of_eq_zero`: over a domain, if additionally `W(œÅ¬±1) ‚âÝ 0`, every zero descends with no case split ‚Äî `W k = 0 ‚üπ W(k‚àíœÅ) = 0` ‚Äî the square absorbing the zero-divisor ambiguity. theorems.name: Ecdlp.NormEDS.normEDS_not_consecutive_zeros`, `normEDS_not_consecutive_zeros'
theorems.claim: normEDS` never has two consecutive zeros ‚Äî Ward apparition rigidity (`normEDS_not_consecutive_zeros` (+ `normEDS_not_consecutive_zeros'` for the `(n‚àí1,n)` pair): over an integral domain, `W = normEDS b c d` never vanishes at both `n` and `n+1`, given `¬¨(b=0 ‚àß c=0)` and `¬¨(c=0 ‚àß d=0)` ‚Äî hypotheses sharp (`(0,0,d)` zeroes `W‚ÇÇ,W‚ÇÉ`; `(b,0,0)` zeroes `W‚ÇÉ,W‚ÇÑ`). Proof: minimal positive zero `œÅ ‚â• 2`; if `W(œÅ+1) ‚âÝ 0` the descent forces every zero index divisible by `œÅ`, so consecutive zeros give `œÅ ‚à£ 1`; if `W(œÅ+1) = 0` then `œÅ ‚àà {2,3}` hits exactly the two excluded degeneracies, and `œÅ ‚â• 4` self-destructs via `normEDS_odd` at `m = œÅ‚àí2`. The scalar heart of node N5 (`gcd(Œ¶‚Çô, Œ®Sq‚Çô) = 1`, `notes/DIVISION_POLY_TORSION_MAP.md`): after the evaluation bridge a common `Œ®Sq n`/`Œ¶ n` root forces exactly such a consecutive pair, and on the curve the two hypotheses are the proved certificates `CoprimePsi2Psi3` / `CoprimePsi3PrePsi4`. Design numerically cross-checked (`scripts/certs/normeds_consecutive_zeros_check.py`))
